docs.title: Polivox Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to Polivox Project Documentation.

docs.title: Folder Structure
docs.text:
• app/: Page routes (Project Manager, Speaker, etc.).
  • components/shared/: Reusable logic like AudioTrackPlayer and StarRating.
  • utils/mock/: Contains the JSON-style arrays for projects, sessions, speakers, and staff.
  • utils/types/: TypeScript interfaces (e.g., interface Session { id: string; status: SessionStatus; ... }).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Technical details for maintaining the Obsidian "Developer" section. Folder Structure UI Standards

docs.title: UI Standards
docs.text:
• Icons: lucide-react only.
  • Notifications: sonner for toast messages (e.g., "Session Flagged Successfully").
  • State Management: Lifted React State. The CreateProject component owns the nodes and edges state, passing it down to the WorkflowBuilder component.

docs.title: Core Capabilities
docs.text:
• Project creation with language pair configuration, topic management, and visual workflow design
  • Speaker and staff database management with 5-star performance ratings and flagging
  • Role-based access control with 6 distinct user roles, each with dedicated dashboards and task queues
  • Multi-stage quality control pipeline (QC1 for initial audio, QC2 for noise-processed audio, Annotation QA)
  • Audio processing pipeline with initial processing and noise injection stages
  • Annotation and transcription workflow with QA review loops
  • Batch export of completed sessions to Google Drive

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Polivox is a web-based platform designed to manage the full lifecycle of bilingual speech data collection. It orchestrates everything from project setup and speaker recruitment to multi-stage quality control (QC), noise processing, and final delivery.

See Core Capabilities for basic overview of capabilities.

See Technology Stack which was used to develop this prototype. (To be finalized for actual project)

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Technology Purpose Next.js (App Router) Web framework (v16.1.6) React UI library (v19.2.3) @xyflow/react Visual workflow builder canvas Tailwind CSS 4 Utility-first styling shadcn/ui Component library (Radix UI + CVA) TypeScript Type-safe development (Strict Mode)

docs.title: Initial Processing QA
docs.text: The Initial Processing QA (often referred to as QC1) is the first human gatekeeper in the workflow, where a Quality Checker (QC) validates the integrity of the raw and standardized audio. The checker uses a specific pass/fail checklist to identify "deal-breakers" like digital clipping, excessive background hiss, crosstalk between microphones, or speakers failing to follow the bilingual language ratio requirements. If the audio fails this stage, the session is flagged and routed back to the Recording node; if it passes, it is cleared for the noise augmentation phase.

docs.title: Initial Processing
docs.text: Once the raw recording is submitted, it enters the Initial Processing stage, handled by the Media Manager (MM). This technical phase focuses on preparing the "clean" audio for downstream tasks by performing non-destructive edits, such as DC offset removal, loudness normalization to a target LUFS level, and channel mapping (ensuring Speaker 1 and Speaker 2 are correctly isolated on Left/Right or separate mono tracks). The goal is to standardize the varied recording environments of different speakers into a uniform technical format before any linguistic analysis or artificial degradation occurs.

docs.title: Language Pairs
docs.text:
In Polivox, a Language Pair is the foundational setting for any project, defining the linguistic scope of the audio data to be collected.

Core Structure A language pair consists of two components:

  • Source Language: The primary language of the conversation.
  • Target Language: The secondary language into which speakers may code-switch or translate during the session.

Matching Requirement The language pair acts as a "filter" for the Speaker Matching Algorithm. For a speaker to be eligible for a session, their Language Profile must include both languages in the pair.

  • Example: If a project is set to EN-ES (English-Spanish), only speakers verified in both English and Spanish will appear in the candidate pool.

docs.title: Noise Processing QA
docs.text: The Noise Processing QA (or QC2) is the final technical audit before the session moves to the Annotator (AN). During this stage, the Quality Checker (QC) ensures that the noise injection was performed correctly and that the speech remains intelligible despite the background interference. The checker verifies that the SNR levels match the project specs and that no artifacts or glitches were introduced during the mixing process. Successfully passing QC2 "locks" the audio files, signaling that the media is technically perfect and ready for full linguistic transcription and tagging.

docs.title: Noise Processing
docs.text: In the Noise Processing stage (also known as Noise Injection), the Media Manager (MM) applies specific environmental audio layers to the clean recordings to simulate real-world AI use cases. Following the project-specific guidelines, the MM mixes the clean speech with designated noise profiles—such as "Street Ambience," "Office Chatter," or "Cafe Background"—at precise Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR). This process is vital for stress-testing speech recognition models, transforming the high-quality studio-standard audio into a diverse dataset of "dirty" audio that mimics spontaneous public conversation.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
Topics define the conversation prompts that speakers will follow during recording sessions. Topics are organized into three categories:

  • Natural Conversation — free-form bilingual conversation
  • Scenario-Based — guided scenarios with specific prompts
  • Interview Style — structured Q&A format

docs.title: Availability Page
docs.text: A standalone page for updating weekly availability at any time. Shows 7 day rows (Monday–Sunday), each with an enable/disable checkbox and start/end time pickers. Default: Mon–Fri 9:00–17:00, weekends disabled. An 'Apply Monday to all' button copies Monday's schedule to all other days. The 'Save Schedule' button persists the changes.

docs.title: Statistics Cards
docs.text:
• Live Topics — Number of topics currently available for recording (with open speaker slots)
  • Upcoming — Scheduled sessions the speaker is expected to join
  • Completed — Total number of successfully recorded sessions
  • Needs Re-record — Sessions flagged by QC that require re-recording (shown in red if > 0)

docs.title: Responsibilities Box
docs.text:
A role information box displays the speaker's core duties:

  • Join the recording room at scheduled time with assigned partner
  • Follow the topic prompt and speak naturally in both languages
  • Ensure microphone is working and background noise is minimal
  • Complete the full recording duration (minimum 5 minutes per session)
  • Re-record sessions if flagged by the QC team

docs.title: Profile Card
docs.text: A personal profile card displays the speaker's avatar (initials), name, country, age range, gender, language badges, and time zone.

docs.title: Live Topics Preview
docs.text: Up to 3 available live topics are shown, each with title, category badge, language pair, and participant slots filled (e.g., '3/6'). Clicking navigates to the full sessions page.

docs.title: Upcoming Sessions Preview
docs.text: Upcoming sessions show topic title, scheduled date/time, language pair, and participant avatars (up to 4 shown with '+N' overflow).

docs.title: Onboarding Page
docs.text: New speakers complete a 4-step onboarding wizard with a visual progress indicator showing completion across steps.

docs.title: Step 1 — Signup
docs.text:
The speaker creates their account with:

  • Email address (read-only, pre-filled from invitation)
  • Full name (required)
  • Password and confirm password (minimum 8 characters, with show/hide toggle)
  • A preview of pre-assigned languages from the invitation (editable in next step)

Validation checks for empty name, short password, and password mismatch.

docs.title: Step 2 — Languages
docs.text:
The speaker selects their working languages:

  • Language A (Main) — required, selected from 12 language options (EN-US, ES-MX, FR-FR, etc.)
  • Language B (Secondary) — optional, shows all languages except the selected Language A
  • Additional Languages — multi-select toggle buttons for all remaining languages
  • A preview box shows the final language configuration

docs.title: Step 3 — Profile Information
docs.text:
• Timezone selection (13 options from America to Australia)
  • Country selection (20 countries)
  • Gender selection (Male, Female, Non-binary, Prefer not to say)
  • Age range selection via interactive buttons (18–30, 30–50, 50+)

The Next button is disabled until all four fields are completed.

docs.title: Step 4 — Availability
docs.text: The speaker sets their weekly availability using the same editor as the standalone Availability page (7 day rows with enable/disable toggles and time pickers). At least one day must be enabled to complete onboarding.

docs.title: Success Screen
docs.text: A congratulations screen with an animated checkmark confirms the setup is complete. A 'Go to Sessions' button navigates to the sessions page.

docs.title: Profile Page
docs.text: Available to all non-PM roles. Displays a profile card (avatar, name, email, role badge, star rating, languages, country, gender) alongside an editable form. Editable fields: Full Name, Phone Number, Timezone. Read-only fields: Email, Languages, Rating, Role (managed by the Project Manager). A 'Save Changes' button persists updates.

docs.title: Sessions Page
docs.text: The sessions page is the speaker's main workspace, organized into two tabs: Live Sessions and Scheduled Sessions.

docs.title: Live Sessions Tab
docs.text:
Displays a responsive grid of topic cards for sessions currently accepting participants. Each card shows:

  • Category badge (color-coded: indigo for Natural Conversation, amber for Scenario-Based, emerald for Interview Style)
  • Topic title
  • Language pair shown as adjacent badges with an arrow between them
  • Participant progress bar showing filled slots (e.g., '3 of 6 speakers'), with a 'FILLING FAST' badge when nearly full
  • 'Join Session' button that navigates to the waiting lobby

docs.title: Waiting Lobby
docs.text:
After clicking 'Join Session', the speaker enters a waiting lobby. The lobby displays:

  • The topic's title, category, and language pair at the top
  • A 6-slot speaker grid showing filled slots (avatar, name, language badge) and empty slots (dashed circle, 'Waiting...')
  • The current speaker's slot is labeled 'You'
  • A status indicator that changes dynamically:
    • While waiting: 'Waiting for speakers to join...' with a clock icon
    • When all 6 slots fill: a 5-second countdown begins automatically with a red pulsing indicator and 'Prepare your microphone...' prompt
    • When countdown reaches 0: 'Starting recording...' with green indicator, then auto-transitions to recording room
  • A 'Leave Queue' button to exit before recording starts

docs.title: Recording Room
docs.text:
The recording room provides a timed 5-minute recording session interface:

  • A pulsing red 'Recording' indicator with microphone icon
  • A large countdown timer (5:00 → 0:00) that changes color: green when > 2 min, yellow at 1–2 min, red when < 30 seconds
  • A conversation script card showing the dialogue lines for the session, with lines advancing automatically based on elapsed time — the current line is highlighted, past lines are dimmed, and future lines are at reduced opacity
  • A speakers section showing all participants as colored avatars with names and language badges
  • An 'End Recording' button (red) to finish early — otherwise recording auto-completes at 5 minutes
  • On completion: a success toast appears ('Recording completed successfully!') and the speaker returns to the browse view

docs.title: Scheduled Sessions Tab
docs.text:
Shows two sections: Upcoming and Past Sessions. Each session card displays:

  • Status badge (Upcoming/blue, Active/green pulsing, Recorded/green check, Rejected/red)
  • A 'Re-record' badge on rejected sessions
  • Topic title and language pair badges
  • Scheduled date and time with calendar icon
  • Participant avatars (up to 4) and speaker count (e.g., '4/6 speakers')
  • A 'Join' button that appears only for upcoming sessions within 15 minutes of start time

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Speaker (SP) are the recording participants who produce bilingual audio content. They have the most interactive experience in the platform — browsing live topics, joining waiting lobbies, participating in timed recording sessions, managing their availability, and completing an onboarding process. The Speaker role also has a personal profile page and a comprehensive dashboard.

Following are the pages of speaker: 10. Speaker - Detailed/Dashboard Sessions Page Onboarding Page Availability Page Profile Page

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: The QC dashboard provides an at-a-glance view of the reviewer's workload across both QC stages.

docs.title: Statistics Cards
docs.text:
• Total Queue — Combined count of all sessions in QC1 and QC2 stages
  • Pending Review — Sessions awaiting initial review across both queues
  • Issues Found — Sessions that have been flagged with problems
  • Total Reviewed — Sessions already advanced past QC stages

docs.title: Queue Previews
docs.text: Two cards show the top 5 sessions in each queue (QC1 and QC2). Each entry displays the topic title, project name, language pair, speaker count, duration, and a status badge (Pending or Issues). A 'View all' link navigates to the full task page.

docs.title: Initial QC (QC1) Page
docs.text: The QC1 page is the full task list for initial audio quality review. It is accessed from the sidebar or the dashboard 'View all' link.

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
The page displays a role information box outlining QC1 duties:

  • Listen to the full mixed audio recording for each session
  • Verify audio is at least 5 minutes long with clear speech from both speakers
  • Check for technical issues: echo, clipping, background noise, or silence gaps
  • Confirm both languages are used appropriately per the topic prompt
  • Flag issues with detailed comments or approve to send to noise processing

docs.title: Statistics & Filters
docs.text: Three stat cards show Total in QC1, Pending Review, and Issues Found. Below, clickable status badge filters (All / Pending Review / Issues Found) let the reviewer narrow the list. Additional dropdowns filter by language pair and topic category, and a search bar supports lookup by topic title, session ID, or category.

docs.title: Session Table
docs.text: A paginated table lists all QC1 sessions with columns for Topic, Session ID, Category, Speakers, Duration (MM:SS), Languages, Tracks, and Status. Each row has a 'Review' button that opens the detail view.

docs.title: QC1 Detail View (Review Modal)
docs.text:
The detail view is a large scrollable modal where the reviewer evaluates a single session. It contains the following sections:

Session Header A sticky header shows the session's topic title, a 'QC 1 — Audio Quality' badge, the recording date and time, session ID, category, language pair, duration, and speaker count.

Previous QC1 Issues Alert If the session was previously flagged and is returning for re-review, an amber warning box displays the original QC1 comments so the reviewer can check whether the reported problems have been fixed.

Mixed Audio Player with Waveform The primary review tool is a mixed audio player with an interactive waveform. The reviewer listens to the combined recording and can drag on the waveform to create 'flagged regions' — timestamped segments that mark where issues occur. Each flagged region appears as a visual marker on the waveform and is listed below the player with its time range (e.g., '0:42 — 1:15') and an editable comment field where the reviewer describes the specific issue. Regions can be individually removed.

Audio Quality Checklist The checklist assigned by Project Manager on Workflow Builder in the Property Panel (Node Configuration) of Quality Checker.

Items turn green with a checkmark icon when completed. A counter shows progress (e.g., '3/5 checked'). All required items must be checked before the 'Good to Go' approval button becomes enabled.

Individual Speaker Tracks A collapsible section shows individual audio players for each speaker's track (e.g., SPK1, SPK2). This helps the reviewer isolate speaker-specific issues like low volume or background noise on a single channel.

Decision Actions The modal footer offers two actions:

  • Good to Go — Approves the session and sends it to the noise processing stage. Disabled until all checklist items are checked.
  • Flag Issues — Opens an issue form with a text area for describing the problems. Any waveform flagged regions are automatically included with their timestamps and comments. Sends the session back to the Media Manager for reprocessing.

docs.title: Noise QC (QC2) Page
docs.text: The QC2 page follows the same structure as QC1 but focuses on validating the noise layer applied to the audio.

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Review the noise-injected audio to ensure environmental sounds are realistic
  • Verify noise levels don't overpower speaker voices
  • Check that noise transitions are smooth and natural
  • Confirm the original speech quality is preserved after noise addition
  • Flag issues for the Media Manager to fix or approve for annotation

docs.title: QC2 Detail View (Review Modal)
docs.text:
The QC2 detail view is structurally similar to QC1 with these key differences:

Noise Layer Information An information card displays the type of noise applied (e.g., 'Office Ambience') and the intensity level (e.g., '20%'), giving the reviewer context for what to expect.

Audio With Noise Player The primary waveform player shows the noise-injected audio. The reviewer can drag to flag problem regions just as in QC1.

Clean Mixed Audio Reference A collapsible section provides the original clean audio (before noise was added) so the reviewer can A/B compare and assess whether the noise layer has degraded the original quality.

Noise Quality Checklist The checklist assigned by Project Manager on Workflow Builder in the Property Panel (Node Configuration) of Quality Checker.

Items turn green with a checkmark icon when completed. A counter shows progress (e.g., '3/5 checked'). All required items must be checked before the 'Good to Go' approval button becomes enabled.

Decision Actions

  • Approve & Send to Annotation — Moves the session to the annotation queue. Requires all checklist items checked.
  • Flag Issue — Opens an issue form and sends the session back to the Media Manager for noise rework, including any flagged waveform regions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Quality Checker (QC) perform two distinct stages of audio quality review. QC1 reviews raw processed audio for technical quality before noise processing. QC2 reviews audio after noise injection to ensure the added noise layer hasn't degraded speech quality or intelligibility. Both stages share a similar workflow: review a queue, open sessions, use checklists and waveform tools, then approve or flag issues.

Following are the pages of Quality Checker: Dashboard Initial QC (QC1) Page Noise QC (QC2) Page

docs.title: Statistics Cards
docs.text:
• Total Queue — Combined count of all sessions in initial and noise processing stages
  • Pending — Sessions awaiting initial processing or noise injection
  • Issues / Rework — Sessions flagged by QC that need reprocessing
  • Completed Today — Sessions successfully processed and submitted today

docs.title: Queue Previews
docs.text: Two cards display the initial processing queue and noise injection queue, each showing up to 5 sessions with topic, project, speakers, languages, and status (Pending or Issues). 'View all' links navigate to the respective full task pages.

docs.title: Initial Processing Page
docs.text: This page is the Media Manager's primary workspace for preparing raw audio recordings.

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Merge individual speaker tracks into a single mixed audio file
  • Normalize audio levels across all speakers for consistent volume
  • Trim silence at the beginning and end of recordings
  • Apply any required audio cleanup (noise reduction, equalization)
  • Submit processed audio to QC1 for quality review

docs.title: Statistics & Filters
docs.text: Three stat cards show Total Sessions, Pending Processing, and Re-processing Required (sessions flagged by QC). Clickable status filters, language and category dropdowns, and a search bar let the MM narrow down the session list.

docs.title: Session Table
docs.text: A paginated table lists sessions with columns for Topic, Session ID, Category, Speakers, Duration, Languages, Tracks, and Status. Sessions flagged by QC show a 'Changes from QC' badge. Each row has an 'Open & Process' button.

docs.title: Initial Processing Detail View
docs.text:
A full-screen modal provides the complete audio processing workspace.

Toolbar Two buttons at the top allow uploading replacement tracks or downloading all tracks as a ZIP archive. The download tooltip shows the ZIP file structure (speaker WAVs, mixed track, and metadata JSON).

QC Feedback Alert If this session is returning from QC with issues, an amber alert box displays the QC reviewer's comments so the MM knows exactly what to fix.

Speaker Tracks Section Individual cards for each speaker track (SPK1, SPK2, etc.) are displayed in a two-column grid. Each card contains:

  • A download and replace button for the individual track
  • A vertical gain slider allowing level adjustment from -20 dB to +20 dB with real-time dB readout
  • An interactive waveform player where the MM can click and drag to select silence regions for removal
  • A list of silenced segments shown as time-range badges (e.g., '0:03 — 0:08') with individual delete buttons and a 'Clear all' option

Normalize Final Mix A dedicated card with a horizontal slider sets the target peak level for the final mix (range: -10 dB to 0 dB). A waveform preview shows the final normalized result.

Final Merged Audio A preview card shows the merged waveform of all speaker tracks combined, with download and replace options.

Submit Action A 'Generate & Send for Quality Check' button at the bottom submits the processed session to QC1. A success toast confirms the submission and the modal closes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Media Manager (MM) handle audio processing at two stages of the pipeline. First, they merge and normalize raw speaker tracks into a clean mixed recording ('Initial Processing'). Then, for 2-speaker projects, they inject synthetic environmental noise into the approved audio ('Noise Processing'). Both stages include tools for waveform editing, level adjustment, silence trimming, and previewing the final output before submitting to QC.

Following are the pages of Media Manager: Dashboard Initial Processing Page Noise Processing Page

docs.title: Noise Processing Page
docs.text: This page is used for adding background noise to approved 2-speaker recordings.

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Select appropriate environmental noise profiles (street, cafe, office, etc.)
  • Mix background noise into the clean audio at realistic levels
  • Ensure noise doesn't mask or distort speaker voices
  • Preview the final mix before submitting
  • Submit noise-processed audio to QC2 for validation

docs.title: Noise Processing Detail View
docs.text:
The detail modal provides a noise mixing workspace.

QC2 Feedback Alert If this session is returning from QC2 with issues, the QC2 reviewer's comments are displayed in an amber alert.

Clean Mixed Audio The approved clean recording is shown with a waveform player for reference, with download and replace options.

Background Noise Layer The MM selects a noise profile using one of two methods:

  • Preset Noise Profiles — Six built-in options displayed as a button grid: Cafe, Street, Office, Public Transport, Restaurant, and Market. Only one can be selected at a time. Presets are disabled when a custom file is uploaded.
  • Custom Noise Upload — A file upload area accepts any audio file as a custom noise layer. When a custom file is selected, its filename is displayed and the preset buttons become dimmed.

Below the noise source selection, an intensity slider (0–100%) controls how much background noise is mixed into the recording.

Live Preview A preview card with a waveform player shows the combined clean audio + noise output. It displays the selected noise type and intensity level, allowing the MM to evaluate the result before submitting.

Submit Action 'Apply Noise & Send for QC2' submits the noise-processed session for quality review.

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Listen to each audio segment and transcribe speaker turns accurately
  • Tag each turn with the correct speaker ID and language code
  • Mark precise start and end timestamps for every segment
  • Identify and label overlapping speech between speakers
  • Follow the annotation guidelines for formatting and language tagging

docs.title: Statistics & Filters
docs.text: Four stat cards (Total Assigned, Pending, Issues, Resubmitted) provide a workload summary. Clickable status filters, language and category dropdowns, and a search bar narrow the session list.

docs.title: Session Table
docs.text: A paginated table with columns for Topic, Session ID, Category, Speakers, Duration, Languages, Tracks, and Status. Sessions with issues show a red badge; resubmitted sessions show a blue badge. Each row has an 'Annotate' button.

docs.title: Annotation Detail View (Editor Modal)
docs.text:
The annotation editor is a large scrollable modal that serves as the annotator's primary workspace. It contains several integrated sections:

Session Header A sticky header shows the topic title, status badges ('Annotation' + 'Needs Revision' or 'Resubmitted' if applicable), recording date, session ID, category, languages, duration, and participant count.

Feedback Thread If the session is returning from QA review, a feedback thread displays all messages exchanged between the annotator and QA reviewer. QA messages appear with a purple background and 'QA Reviewer' badge; annotator messages appear with a blue background and 'Annotator' badge. Each message may include linked turns displayed as amber badges — clicking a turn badge scrolls to and highlights that turn in the editor below. The annotator can reply to QA feedback by typing a message and clicking Send.

Mixed Audio Player A full audio player with waveform visualization shows the combined recording. The annotator listens while creating turns, using play/pause controls, a progress bar, and elapsed/remaining time display.

Individual Speaker Tracks A collapsible section shows separate audio players for each speaker's track. This helps the annotator identify individual speakers when transcribing and resolve ambiguous segments.

Transcript Reference A collapsible section displays an auto-generated reference transcript (if available). Each segment shows a timestamp, a color-coded speaker name, and the segment text in a speech bubble. A speaker legend with colored dots is shown at the bottom. This reference is read-only and serves as a starting point for annotation.

Annotation Turns Editor The main editing area where the annotator creates and manages individual transcription turns. The toolbar offers two options:

  • AI Transcribe — Auto-generates annotation turns from the reference transcript, pre-filling speaker IDs, timing, languages, and text. A toast confirms the action.
  • Add Turn — Manually adds a new blank turn below the last one, with the start time set to the previous turn's end time.

Each turn is a collapsible card with the following editable fields:

  • Turn label (e.g., Turn 1, Turn 2) — automatically numbered
  • Speaker dropdown (SPK1, SPK2, ... based on participant count)
  • Language dropdown (language codes extracted from the session)
  • Start time and End time inputs in MM:SS format
  • Transcription text area for the spoken content
  • Delete button to remove the turn (remaining turns are renumbered)

Turns that were flagged by QA are visually highlighted with an amber border and a 'QA flagged' badge, making it immediately clear which turns need attention.

Submission A 'Submit for QC' button (disabled if no turns exist) sends the annotation to the Annotator QA reviewer. A success toast confirms the submission.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Annotator (AN) work on transcription and linguistic annotation of QC-approved audio sessions. They produce turn-by-turn transcriptions with speaker identification, language tags, and precise timing information. The annotation interface includes audio playback, a reference transcript, and a full turn editor with feedback integration for QA review loops.

Following are the pages of Annotator: Dashboard Annotation Tasks Page Annotator Workflow

docs.title: Annotator Workflow
docs.text:
The typical annotator workflow is:

  1. Open a pending session from the task list
  2. Listen to the mixed audio and reference individual speaker tracks as needed
  3. Use 'AI Transcribe' to auto-populate turns from the reference transcript, or add turns manually
  4. Edit each turn: correct the speaker, language, timestamps, and transcription text
  5. Submit for QA review
  6. If QA sends back feedback, review the flagged turns (highlighted in amber), read QA comments, make corrections, and resubmit

docs.title: Statistics Cards
docs.text:
• Total Assigned — All sessions assigned to the annotator across all statuses
  • Pending — Sessions awaiting initial annotation
  • Issues — Sessions sent back by QA with feedback requiring revision
  • Completed — Sessions that have passed QA and moved to delivery

docs.title: Annotation Queue
docs.text: A card shows the top 5 sessions in the annotation queue with topic, project, languages, speakers, duration, and status (Pending, Issues, or Resubmitted). A 'View all' link navigates to the full task page.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Annotator QA reviewers verify the accuracy and completeness of annotations created by Annotators. They serve as the final quality gate before sessions become ready for export. The QA interface provides read-only annotation viewing, turn-level feedback tagging, overlap detection, and a quality checklist.

Following are the pages of Annotator QA: Dashboard QA Tasks Page QA Review Workflow

docs.title: Statistics Cards
docs.text:
• Total Queue — All sessions in annotation QA stages
  • Pending QA — Sessions awaiting first QA review
  • Resubmitted — Sessions that annotators have fixed and resubmitted
  • Approved — Sessions that have passed QA and are ready for delivery

docs.title: QA Review Queue
docs.text: A card shows the top 5 sessions with topic, project, languages, duration, and status (Pending QA or Resubmitted).

docs.title: QA Review Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open a pending session from the QA task list
  2. Listen to the mixed audio while reviewing the annotation turns table
  3. Run the overlap detection check to identify potential issues
  4. Click problematic turns to select them for feedback
  5. Either: write feedback referencing the selected turns (session returns to annotator) or check all quality items and approve
  6. If the annotator resubmits, re-review the updated annotation

docs.title: Page Header & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Spot-check annotation accuracy against the original audio
  • Verify speaker IDs and language tags are correctly assigned
  • Check timestamp precision and segment boundaries
  • Run overlap detection and validate flagged segments
  • Approve accurate annotations or send back with specific feedback

docs.title: Statistics & Filters
docs.text: Three stat cards (Total in QA, Pending QA, Resubmitted) summarize the queue. Clickable status filters, language and category dropdowns, and search bar provide filtering.

docs.title: QA Detail View (Review Modal)
docs.text:
The QA review modal provides tools for thorough annotation verification.

Session Header A sticky header shows the topic title, 'Annotation QA' badge, optional 'Resubmitted' badge, recording date, session ID, and session metadata. Also displays the count of annotation turns for quick reference.

Mixed Audio & Speaker Tracks Same audio playback tools as the annotator view — a mixed audio player and collapsible individual speaker tracks — so the reviewer can listen while verifying annotations.

Annotation Turns Table (Read-Only) All annotation turns are displayed in a read-only table with columns for turn number, speaker, start time, end time, language, and transcription. Unlike the annotator's editable view, the QA reviewer cannot modify turns — they can only select them for feedback. Clicking a row selects it (highlighted with an amber left border and amber background). Multiple turns can be selected simultaneously, building a list of problematic turns to reference in feedback.

Feedback Thread The feedback system allows the QA reviewer to communicate with the annotator at a granular turn level:

  • The reviewer selects problematic turns by clicking rows in the annotation table
  • Selected turns appear as removable amber badges in the feedback composer (e.g., 'Turn 3', 'Turn 7')
  • The reviewer writes a description of the issues in a text area
  • Clicking 'Send' creates a feedback message with the turn references attached, and returns the session to the annotator
  • All previous messages are displayed in the thread — QA messages (purple) and annotator replies (blue)
  • Clicking a turn badge in any message jumps to and highlights that turn in the table for 2 seconds

Overlap Detection A built-in analysis tool detects overlapping speech segments. The reviewer clicks 'Run Overlap Check' to trigger a scan. After a brief analysis, results are displayed showing:

  • Time range of each detected overlap (e.g., '1:23 — 1:28')
  • The speakers involved (e.g., 'SPK1 + SPK2')
  • An overlap type badge: 'Real Overlap' (amber) or 'False Positive' (gray)
  • A toggle button to reclassify each overlap between real and false positive

A summary at the bottom shows the total real overlaps and false positives.

Annotation Quality Checklist A four-item checklist ensures systematic evaluation:

  • Accurate turn boundaries (start/end times)
  • Correct speaker labels (SPK1–SPK6)
  • Correct language tags per turn
  • Acceptable transcription quality

All items must be checked to enable the approval button. A counter shows progress and an 'All passed' badge appears when complete.

Decision Actions

  • Approve — No Fixes Required — Moves the session to ready-for-delivery status. Disabled until all checklist items are checked.
  • Send Feedback (via thread) — Returns the session to the annotator with linked turn references. The annotator sees which specific turns need attention.

docs.title: Annotator (AN)
docs.text:
Annotators work on transcription and linguistic annotation of approved audio sessions. They produce turn-by-turn transcriptions with speaker identification, language labels, and timing information.

Responsibilities

  • Transcribe bilingual audio sessions with accurate speaker identification
  • Add linguistic annotations: language labels, timing markers, entity tags
  • Submit completed annotations for QA review
  • Address feedback and resubmit annotations when issues are identified by QA

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard Annotation queue overview with pending and issue counts Annotation Tasks /annotation-tasks Annotation workspace with audio-synced transcript editing

docs.title: Annotator QA (AQ)
docs.text:
Annotator QA reviewers verify the accuracy and completeness of annotations produced by Annotators. They serve as the final quality gate before sessions become ready for export.

Responsibilities

  • Review transcriptions and annotations for accuracy and completeness
  • Verify speaker identification, language labels, and timing accuracy
  • Approve annotations that meet quality standards
  • Request fixes with specific feedback when issues are found, linking to problematic turns

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard QA queue overview with pending and resubmitted counts QA Tasks /qa-tasks QA review workspace: review annotations, approve or request fixes

docs.title: Media Manager (MM)
docs.text:
Media Managers handle audio processing tasks at two stages: initial processing of raw recordings (format conversion, normalization) and noise injection (adding synthetic background noise for data augmentation).

Responsibilities

  • Process raw audio recordings: format conversion, level normalization, audio cleanup, mix multiple audio tracks into single audio. See Initial Processing
    • Can download and upload any single or mixed audio tracks
  • Submit initially processed sessions to QC1 for quality review
  • Inject synthetic noise into approved audio files for data augmentation. See Noise Processing
  • Submit noise-processed sessions to QC2 for quality review
  • Handle reprocessing when QC identifies issues with initial processing or noise injection

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard Processing queue overview with initial and noise queues Initial Processing /media-library Process raw audio, view details, submit to QC1 Noise Processing /noise-processing Add noise to audio, view details, submit to QC2

docs.title: Project Manager (PM)
docs.text:
The Project Manager has the highest-level view of the platform and oversees the entire data production lifecycle. They are the only role that can create and edit projects, manage team composition, and export final deliverables.

Responsibilities

  • Create and configure projects (name, description, language pairs, topics, workflow)
  • Assign speakers and staff to projects from the platform database
  • Monitor session progress through the entire production pipeline
  • Review sessions that have been flagged with issues at any QC or annotation stage.
  • Make disposition decisions: send sessions for re-recording, reprocessing, or advance to next phase
  • Evaluate speaker performance with star ratings and optional flagging
  • Export completed and approved sessions to Google Drive
  • Manage the speaker and staff databases (add, edit, delete, rate, flag). See details in Speaker & Staff Management

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard Overview stats, session pipeline, projects, recent activity, sessions needing attention Projects /projects Create, edit, manage projects and view sessions Save to Drive /save-to-drive Configure Google Drive and export approved sessions Speakers /speakers Manage speaker database (CRUD, rate, flag, import) Staff /staff Manage staff database (CRUD, rate, flag, import)

docs.title: Quality Checker (QC)
docs.text:
Quality Checkers perform two distinct stages of audio quality review. QC1 reviews raw processed audio for quality issues, while QC2 reviews audio after noise injection to ensure the noise layer hasn't degraded quality.

Responsibilities

  • Review initial audio recordings for quality issues (QC1): audio levels, clarity, background noise, correct language usage. See Initial Processing QA
  • Review noise-injected audio for quality issues (QC2): noise levels, speech intelligibility, artifact detection. See Noise Processing QA
  • Approve sessions that meet quality standards to advance in the pipeline
  • Flag sessions with issues and provide detailed comments for the Media Manager or Project Manager
  • Follow QC checklists defined in the project workflow

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard QC queue overview with stats and pending session lists Initial QC /initial-qc QC1 audio review: listen, evaluate checklist, approve/flag Noise QC /noise-qc QC2 noise review: listen, evaluate, approve/flag

docs.title: Speaker (SP)
docs.text:
Speakers are the recording participants who produce bilingual audio content. They browse available topics, join recording sessions with partner speakers, and can manage their availability and profile.

Responsibilities

  • Complete the onboarding process (account setup, language selection, profile info, availability)
  • Browse live topics and join recording sessions with assigned partners
  • Speak naturally in both languages following topic prompts
  • Ensure microphone quality and minimize background noise
  • Complete full recording duration (minimum 5 minutes per session)
  • Re-record sessions if flagged by the QC team
  • Set and update weekly availability hours

Available Pages

Page Path Purpose Dashboard /dashboard Personal stats, profile card, live topics, upcoming sessions Sessions /sessions Browse live topics, join waiting lobby, enter recording room, view past sessions Availability /availability Set weekly available hours per day Onboarding /onboarding 4-step onboarding: signup, languages, profile, availability

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The platform is built around six specialized roles, each managing a specific phase of the production pipeline.

  1. Project Manager (PM)
  2. Speaker (SP)
  3. Quality Checker (QC)
  4. Media Manager (MM)
  5. Annotator (AN)
  6. Annotator QA (AQ)

You can also view User Roles - Summary for quick overview of roles.

docs.title: User Roles Summary
docs.text:
Polivox defines six specialized user roles. Each is responsible for a distinct phase of the speech data production pipeline. The table below gives a quick comparison; the sections that follow describe each role's pages and workflows in full detail.

Role Key Responsibility Pages Project Manager Oversee projects, manage teams, review issues, export Dashboard, Projects, Speakers, Staff, Save to Drive Quality Checker Two-stage audio quality review (QC1 + QC2) Dashboard, Initial QC, Noise QC Speaker Record bilingual conversations Dashboard, Sessions, Availability, Onboarding Media Manager Audio processing and noise injection Dashboard, Initial Processing, Noise Processing Annotator Transcribe and annotate approved audio Dashboard, Annotation Tasks Annotator QA Review annotation accuracy and completeness Dashboard, QA Tasks Capability PM QC Speaker MM Annotator Ann. QA Create / Edit Projects Yes — — — — — Manage Staff & Speakers Yes — — — — — Export to Google Drive Yes — — — — — Record Sessions — — Yes — — — Review Audio Quality — Yes — — — — Process / Mix Audio — — — Yes — — Annotate Transcripts — — — — Yes — QA Annotations — — — — — Yes Profile Page — Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The prototype uses a demo-role selection screen instead of a traditional backend auth system.

  • Role Storage: Selected role is stored in localStorage as demoRole.
  • Access Control: The demoRole determines the sidebar navigation options and page-level features available to the user.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Project Creation Wizard is a multi-step process used by the Project Manager (PM) to define the parameters of a new data collection effort. Step 1: Basic Information Step 2: Speaker Management Step 3: Topic Management Step 4: Workflow Builder

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text:
• Project Name (Required Field): Unique identifier for the project.
  • Description (Optional Field): A short description about project.
  • Language Pairs (Required Field): Selection of Source and Target languages (e.g., English-Spanish). The language pairs added here will be set for each topic by default.

docs.title: Speaker Management
docs.text:
• Project Manager (PM) can select already added speakers from the list. System will send them invite for the project.
    • Languages of each speaker is displayed.
    • Rating of each speaker is displayed.

In addition to selecting already added speakers, Project Manager (PM) can also add a speaker manually. In this case, system will send Sign Up link on speakers email.

Fields are:

  • Speaker Name (Required Field): Name of the speaker.
  • Email (Required Field): Email of the speaker on which to send Sign Up link.
  • Languages (Required Field): All languages which speaker can speak separated with comma.
    • First language will be set as Primary Language in Speaker Profile.
    • Second language will be set as Secondary Language in Speaker Profile.
    • Others will be set in additional languages in Speaker Profile.

Also speakers can be added using excel file. System will import all users from excel file:

  • Will send Invite Link to the speakers which already exists in database.
  • Will send Sign Up Link to the speakers which does not exist in database.

The format of excel file is as follows:

  • Speaker ID will be removed in actual app.
Name Email Role Speaker ID LanguageA LanguageB LanguageC_list Sarah Martinez sarah@example.com recorder SPK1 EN-US ES-MX Carlos Rodriguez carlos@example.com recorder SPK2 ES-MX EN-US FR-FR Marie Dubois marie@example.com recorder - FR-FR EN-US James Thompson james@example.com recorder SPK4 EN-US Emma Wilson emma@example.com recorder - EN-US Lucas Torres lucas@example.com recorder SPK6 ES-MX Ana Silva ana@example.com recorder SPK7 PT-BR ES-MX EN-US Diego Perez diego@example.com recorder SPK8 ES-MX EN-US Sophie Laurent sophie@example.com recorder - EN-US Maria Lopez maria@example.com recorder SPK10 ES-MX PT-BR John Smith john@example.com recorder SPK11 EN-US FR-FR, PT-BR Gabriela Santos gabriela@example.com recorder SPK12 EN-US

docs.title: Topic Management
docs.text:
Projects are organized around specific conversation Topics to ensure lexical diversity.

  • Topic Input: PMs add topics (e.g., "Financial Planning," "Travel Logistics").
  • Here, languages pairs are assigned by default by system which were added in Basic Information
    • A language pair can be removed from a specific topic by clicking on that language pair in the topic.

Topics can be either added manually or a list can be uploaded from a txt file.

Format of txt file is as follows:

[TOPIC] Daily Life & Routines
SCENARIO: Tell me about your typical morning routine. What time do you usually wake up? What's the first thing you do?

[TOPIC] Restaurant Experience
SCENARIO: Role-play ordering food at a restaurant with dietary restrictions. One person is the waiter, the other is a customer with allergies.

[TOPIC] Career Discussion
SCENARIO: Discuss your career path and professional goals for the future. What inspired you to choose your field? Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

docs.title: Workflow Builder
docs.text: This is the core of the Polivox prototype. Instead of a hardcoded 2-person or 6-person flow, the PM designs the logic on a visual canvas.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The workflow builder is composed of four main components that work together:

Component File Purpose StepWorkflowBuilder StepWorkflowBuilder.tsx Main orchestrator — manages state, coordinates sub-components WorkflowNodePalette WorkflowNodePalette.tsx Left sidebar — draggable node type cards WorkflowCanvas WorkflowCanvas.tsx Center — React Flow canvas for visual editing WorkflowPropertyPanel WorkflowPropertyPanel.tsx Right sidebar — node configuration panel WorkflowNode nodes/WorkflowNode.tsx Custom node rendering (standard + condition diamond)

The layout is a three-panel design: the palette on the left (224px), the interactive canvas in the center (flexible width), and the property panel on the right (320px, shown only when a node is selected).

docs.title: Canvas Interactions
docs.text:
The canvas is the central interactive area where nodes are placed and connected. It supports:

  • Drag-and-Drop Placement — Nodes from the palette are dropped onto the canvas at the desired position
  • Node Selection — Clicking a node selects it and opens the property panel on the right. Clicking the canvas background deselects.
  • Edge Drawing — Dragging from an output handle (right side of node) to an input handle (left side of another node) creates a connection edge.
  • Pan and Zoom — The canvas supports mouse wheel zoom and click-drag panning.
  • Minimap — A small overview map in the bottom-right corner for navigation in large workflows.
  • Background Grid — A dot grid with 16px spacing provides visual alignment reference.

Default edge styling uses gray stroke (#94a3b8), 2px width, with animation. Edges from condition nodes use path-specific colors and labels.

docs.title: Condition Branching System
docs.text: The condition node is the most complex node type, enabling multi-path workflow branching. It allows the workflow to diverge into different processing paths based on a runtime field value.

docs.title: 1. Configuration
docs.text:
A condition node is configured with:

  • Branch Field — The data field to evaluate. Options: Speaker Count, Language Pair, Recording Type, or Custom Field.
  • Paths — Each path defines a possible output branch with:
    • Label — display name (e.g., '2 Speakers')
    • Value — the matching value (e.g., '2')
    • Color — automatically assigned from 8-color palette: Emerald, Blue, Amber, Red, Violet, Pink, Cyan, Lime

docs.title: 2. How Branching Works
docs.text:
When a condition node has paths configured:

  • Each path creates a separate output handle on the right side of the diamond
  • Handles are color-coded and labeled to distinguish branches
  • When a user draws an edge from a specific path handle to a target node, the edge inherits that path's color and label
  • This creates a visually clear multi-path workflow where each branch is identifiable by color

Example: A condition node branching on 'Speaker Count' might have three paths — '2 Speakers' (green), '6 Speakers' (blue), '10 Speakers' (amber) — each leading to different downstream processing nodes.

docs.title: Node Data Structure
docs.text:
Every workflow node stores its configuration in a WorkflowNodeData object with the following fields:

  • role — The node type identifier (e.g., 'speaker', 'quality-checker', 'condition')
  • label — User-editable display name for the node
  • description — Optional description text (not available for condition nodes)
  • tasks — Array of task/responsibility strings assigned to this node
  • acceptableSpeakerCounts — Recording Room only — array of valid speaker counts (e.g., [2, 6, 10])
  • qcSubtype — Quality Checker only — 'initial-processing-qa' or 'noise-qa'
  • mmSubtype — Media Manager only — 'initial-processing' or 'noise-processing'
  • checklist — Quality Checker only — array of checklist items (id, label, required flag)
  • conditionConfig — Condition only — branching field and path definitions
  • assignedStaff — Array of assigned staff members (id, name, role)

docs.title: Node Palette (Drag Source)
docs.text:
The left sidebar displays all eight node types as draggable cards. Each card shows the node's icon (color-coded), label, and a short description. A grip handle icon indicates that the card is draggable.

To add a node to the workflow, the user drags a card from the palette and drops it onto the canvas. The system:

  • Captures the palette item data via HTML5 drag-and-drop (MIME type: application/workflow-node)
  • Converts the mouse drop position from screen coordinates to React Flow canvas coordinates
  • Creates a new WorkflowNode with a unique ID (format: node-{counter}-{timestamp})
  • Initializes the node with default data (empty tasks, default condition config for condition nodes)

docs.title: 1. Standard Nodes
docs.text:
Standard nodes (all types except Condition) render as rounded rectangles with a colored left border matching the node type. The content includes:

  • Icon in a colored square (top-left) with the node label beside it
  • Optional description text below the label (gray, truncated)
  • Subtype badge for QC and MM nodes (e.g., 'Initial Processing QA')
  • Recording Room: 'Accepts: 2, 6, 10 speakers' badge showing valid counts
  • Checklist indicator for QC nodes (e.g., '3 checklist items')
  • Assigned staff indicator showing first 2 names + '+N more'
  • Tasks preview showing first 3 items + '+N more'

Handles: one input handle on the left (target) and one output handle on the right (source), both colored to match the node type.

docs.title: 2. Condition Nodes
docs.text:
Condition nodes have a distinctive diamond shape (rotated square) to visually distinguish them from standard nodes. They display:

  • GitBranch icon in a colored circle (center)
  • Node label (bold, centered)
  • Branching field name (e.g., 'speakerCount')
  • Path count indicator (e.g., '3 paths')

The diamond dynamically grows taller based on the number of paths (minimum height: 140px, formula: max(140, pathCount × 36 + 80)). Each path creates a separate colored output handle on the right side, distributed vertically with equal spacing. Path labels are displayed next to their handles.

See Condition Branching System for more info.

docs.title: Property Panel (Node Configuration)
docs.text: When a node is selected, the right sidebar (320px) opens to show all configurable properties for that node type. The panel adapts its content based on the node's role.

docs.title: Universal Properties (All Nodes)
docs.text:
• Label — text input to customize the node's display name
  • Description — optional text input (hidden for condition nodes)

docs.title: Recording Room Properties
docs.text: • Acceptable Speaker Counts — A numeric input with add/remove functionality. Values appear as removable badges. Defines which speaker configurations are valid for this recording room (e.g., exactly 2, 6, or 10 speakers).

docs.title: Quality Checker Properties
docs.text:
• QC Subtype — Dropdown to select 'Initial Processing QA' or 'Noise Processing QA'.
  • QC Checklist — Add custom verification items. Each item has a label and a required/optional toggle. QC reviewers use this checklist when evaluating sessions. See Initial QC (QC1) Page where this checklists will be visible.

docs.title: Media Manager Properties
docs.text: • Processing Type — Dropdown to select 'Initial Processing' or 'Noise Processing'.

docs.title: Condition Node Properties
docs.text:
• Branch On — Dropdown to select the branching field (Speaker Count, Language Pair, Recording Type, Custom).
  • Path Management — Add new paths (label + value), edit existing path labels, remove paths. Each path automatically receives a color from the 8-color palette.

docs.title: Tasks/Responsibilities (Non-Condition Nodes)
docs.text: • A free-form list of tasks. Add task descriptions via a text input. Each task appears as a removable item. The first 3 tasks are previewed on the node card.

docs.title: Delete Node
docs.text: • A red destructive button at the bottom of every property panel allows deleting the selected node and all its connected edges.

docs.title: Staff Assignment
docs.text: Nodes representing human roles can have staff members assigned to them. This is how project managers allocate team members to specific workflow stages.

docs.title: Eligible Node Types
docs.text:
• Media Manager (MM)
  • Quality Checker (QC)
  • Annotator (AN)
  • Annotator QA (AQ)

docs.title: Assignment Process
docs.text:
• The property panel shows an 'Assign Role' button for eligible nodes
  • Clicking opens a searchable dialog showing staff from the database filtered by matching role
  • Each staff member card displays: initials avatar, name, email, star rating, language badges
  • Already-assigned staff show an 'Assigned' badge
  • Clicking a staff card toggles their assignment on/off
  • Multiple staff can be assigned to a single node
  • Assigned staff appear in the property panel with individual remove buttons
  • The node card preview shows the first 2 assigned names and '+N more' for additional staff

docs.title: State Management & Data Flow
docs.text:
The workflow builder's state is managed through React Flow hooks (useNodesState, useEdgesState) inside StepWorkflowBuilder. The data flow works as follows:

  • Node Creation: Palette drag → Canvas drop → new node added to state
  • Node Update: Property panel change → handleNodeUpdate → updates both nodes array and selectedNode
  • Node Delete: Delete button → removes node and all connected edges → deselects
  • Edge Creation: Handle drag → onConnect → evaluates condition paths → applies styling → adds edge
  • Sync to Parent: When property panel closes → syncToParent → onChange callback sends current nodes/edges to CreateProject parent component

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Workflow Builder is the most sophisticated component of the Polivox platform. It provides a visual, node-and-edge canvas for designing the processing pipeline that each recording session will follow. Built on React Flow (@xyflow/react v12), it supports drag-and-drop node creation, multi-path conditional branching, per-node staff assignment, and comprehensive property configuration.

Architecture Overview Workflow Node Types Node Data Structure Node Palette (Drag Source) Canvas Interactions Node Visual Rendering Condition Branching System Property Panel (Node Configuration) State Management & Data Flow

docs.title: Workflow Node Types
docs.text:
Each node represents a state in the Session Management & Status Flow.

The system defines eight distinct node types, each representing a different role or function in the processing pipeline:

Node Type Icon Color Description Speaker Mic Indigo (#6366f1) Language speaker participant in recording sessions Recording Room Radio Sky Blue (#0ea5e9) Room configuration where speakers record Media Manager FileAudio Amber (#f59e0b) Audio file processing (initial or noise) Quality Checker ShieldCheck Emerald (#10b981) Audio and data quality review Annotator PenTool Violet (#8b5cf6) Transcription and linguistic annotation Annotator QA ClipboardCheck Pink (#ec4899) Annotation accuracy review Processing Step Cpu Slate (#64748b) Automated processing (noise reduction, etc.) Condition GitBranch Orange (#f97316) Branch workflow based on dynamic conditions

docs.title: Edit Project Dialog
docs.text:
The Edit Project dialog provides a tabbed interface for modifying project configuration:

  • Details Tab — Edit project description (name is read-only after creation)
  • Language Pairs Tab — Add new pairs, edit existing pairs (both languages), remove pairs. Removing a pair also removes it from all topic-LP assignments.
  • Topics Tab — Add new topics, delete topics, toggle language pair assignments per topic. Language pair badges can be clicked to toggle assignment for each topic individually.

docs.title: Manage Team Dialog
docs.text:
A separate dialog for managing the human resources assigned to a project:

  • Speakers Tab — Search and multi-select speakers from the database. Display shows initials, name, languages, and star rating. Selected speakers shown in a badges list with remove buttons.
  • Staff Tab — Search and multi-select staff from the database. Display shows initials, name, role (color-coded badge), and star rating. Selected staff shown with role badges and remove buttons.

docs.title: Project List
docs.text: The project list view displays all projects in a searchable, filterable table. Each row shows the project name, ID, assigned staff count, session progress (completed/total with progress bar), completion percentage, and creation date.

docs.title: Filtering & Search
docs.text:
• Text search by project name or ID
  • Filters
  • Pagination with configurable page size (default 10 per page)

docs.title: Actions (Row Dropdown Menu)
docs.text:
• Edit Project — opens the Edit Project dialog
  • Manage Staff & Speakers — opens the team management dialog
  • Export to Drive — visible only for projects with completed sessions

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: See these links for detailed info on project management. Project List Edit Project Dialog Manage Team Dialog

docs.title: Complete Session Status Flow
docs.text:
This flow might change for different projects.

Sessions progress through up to 24 distinct statuses representing the full production pipeline. The primary flow is:

Status Phase Responsible Role recorded Recording Speaker initial-processing Audio Processing Media Manager reprocessing-required Audio Processing (rework) Media Manager qc1-pending Quality Control 1 Quality Checker qc1-approved QC1 Passed — qc1-issues QC1 Issues (PM Review) Project Manager noise-processing Noise Injection Media Manager noise-reprocessing Noise Rework Media Manager qc2-pending Quality Control 2 Quality Checker qc2-approved QC2 Passed — qc2-issues QC2 Issues (PM Review) Project Manager annotation-pending Annotation Annotator annotation-issues Annotation Issues (PM Review) Project Manager annotation-resubmitted Annotator Resubmitted Annotator QA annotation-qc-pending Annotation QA Review Annotator QA annotation-qc-approved Annotation QA Passed — ready-for-delivery Ready for Export Project Manager exported Exported to Drive — rejected Rejected — re-record-required Re-recording Needed Speaker

docs.title: Workflow Differences by Project Type
docs.text:
• 2-Speaker Projects — Include the noise injection step. Sessions go through QC2 after noise processing.
  • 6-Speaker Projects — Skip the noise injection step entirely. Sessions move directly from QC1 to annotation.

docs.title: Session Detail View
docs.text: Clicking a session row opens a detailed modal with three view modes based on session state:

docs.title: 1. Active View
docs.text:
• Current pipeline status indicator showing which phase the session is in
  • Assigned staff information (name, ID, role)
  • Individual speaker audio tracks (collapsible, with audio player per speaker)
  • Mixed audio track (highlighted center focus)

docs.title: 2. Review View (Sessions with Issues)
docs.text:
• Review messages from QC/Annotation QA staff with their name, ID, role, and feedback text
  • Mixed audio track for PM review
  • Transcript viewer (if annotation-issues status)
  • Individual speaker tracks for detailed review

PM Actions:

  • Send For Re-Recording — Select rejection category (Audio Quality, Wrong Language, Technical Issue, Content Quality, Incomplete, Other). Evaluate individual speakers with star ratings and optional flagging. Add notes and a PM message. Option to require re-record with same participants.
  • Send To Next Phase — Add instructions and confirm to advance the session past the issue.

docs.title: 3. Past View (Completed Sessions)
docs.text:
• Full production timeline with staff names, IDs, roles, dates, and status badges
  • Google Drive export location with link
  • Individual speaker tracks and final mixed audio

docs.title: Session List View
docs.text:
When a project is selected from the project list, the session list view displays all sessions for that project organized in three tabs:

  • Active Tab — Sessions currently in progress (not exported, not in review). Shows the normal pipeline flow.
  • PM Review Tab — Sessions that have been flagged with issues at any QC or annotation stage (qc1-issues, qc2-issues, annotation-issues). These require PM disposition.
  • Completed Tab — Sessions that have been exported to Google Drive.

docs.title: Session Table Columns
docs.text:
• Topic title, Category (Natural Conversation / Scenario-Based / Interview Style)
  • Languages (Language A — Language B)
  • Participants (avatar circles with initials, +N overflow)
  • Duration (in seconds), Status badge (color-coded), Recorded date

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: For detailed info see these links: Session List View Session Detail View Complete Session Status Flow

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
These pages are managed by Project Manager (PM)

See these links for detailed info: Speaker Management Staff Management

docs.title: Speaker Management
docs.text: The Speaker Management page (/speakers) allows the Project Manager (PM) to maintain a comprehensive database of Speaker (SP).

docs.title: Table Display
docs.text: • Avatar initials, Name, Email, Languages (L1/L2/L3 badges), Country, Gender, Star Rating, Flag button

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
• Search by name or email
  • Filter by country, language, star rating (1-5), flag status (All / Flagged / Not Flagged)

docs.title: CRUD Operations
docs.text:
• Add Speaker — Manual entry (name, email, languages, country, gender, rating) or bulk import from .xlsx/.csv
  • View Speaker — Detail modal showing all info with in-modal rating adjustment, flag toggle, and edit/delete actions
  • Edit Speaker — Form modal to modify all speaker fields
  • Delete Speaker — Confirmation dialog with speaker name shown
  • Flag Speaker — Toggle flag status for performance tracking
  • Rate Speaker — Interactive 5-star rating widget (1-5 scale)

docs.title: Staff Management
docs.text: The Staff Management page (/staff) mirrors the speaker management interface for staff members.

docs.title: Staff Roles (Color-Coded)
docs.text:
• Media Manager (MM) (blue badge)
  • Quality Checker (QC) (emerald badge)
  • Annotator (AN) (violet badge)
  • Annotator QA (AQ) (amber badge)

docs.title: CRUD Operations
docs.text: Identical to speaker management: add (manual or file import), view, edit, delete, flag, and rate. The key difference is that staff entries include a role assignment that determines which workflow nodes they can be assigned to.

docs.title: Export Deliverables
docs.text:
Each exported session generates two files:

  • 1 mixed audio WAV file (the combined recording from all speakers)
  • 1 JSON annotation file (containing transcriptions, metadata, speaker info, timing data)

After export, sessions transition to the 'exported' status and are removed from the export queue.

docs.title: Export Options
docs.text:
• Individual Review — Click a session row to open a review dialog with audio and metadata before exporting
  • Bulk Export — Select multiple sessions and click 'Export N Sessions' to batch export
  • Project Export — When filtering by a specific project, an 'Export Project' button exports all ready sessions for that project at once

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Save to Drive page (/save-to-drive) enables Project Managers to export completed sessions to Google Drive storage.

Google Drive Configuration Session Filtering & Selection Export Options Export Deliverables

docs.title: Google Drive Configuration
docs.text:
• Enter a Google Drive folder ID to configure the export destination
  • Visual status indicator shows whether Drive is configured
  • Configuration can be changed at any time
  • Export buttons are disabled until Drive is configured

docs.title: Session Filtering & Selection
docs.text:
• Only sessions with 'ready-for-delivery' status are shown
  • Filter by project, category, or search by topic title/session ID
  • Checkbox selection per session with Select All / Deselect All options
  • Selected count displayed (e.g., '3 of 8 selected')

docs.title: Matching Paradigms
docs.text:
The algorithm supports different matching modes depending on the project type:

  • Bilingual Pairing: Finds two speakers who are both fluent in the same Language Pair (e.g., both speak English and Spanish).
  • Expert-to-Learner: (If configured) Pairs a Native speaker with a Learner for specific pedagogical data collection.
  • N-Person Grouping: For 6-person workflows, the algorithm clusters speakers based on shared language commonalities to ensure fluid conversation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The algorithm identifies and pairs two or more speakers for a specific session. It follows a "Filtering-to-Ranking" logic to ensure the highest quality data collection.

The Selection Logic (The Three Filters) Matching Paradigms The Ranking & Scoring System

docs.title: The Ranking & Scoring System
docs.text:
Once a pool of eligible speakers is found, Polivox ranks them using a Priority Score (S):

$S=(R×0.6)+(C×0.4)$

Where:

  • R (Rating): The speaker's average 5-star rating.
  • C (Completion): A ratio of successfully completed sessions vs. flagged/rejected sessions.

Higher S values are matched first to ensure that the most reliable speakers handle the bulk of the project's target sessions.

docs.title: The Selection Logic (The Three Filters)
docs.text:
A speaker must pass through three distinct logical gates to be considered for a "Match":

  1. Linguistic Gate: * The speaker’s Language Profile must contain the Source and Target languages defined in the Project Creation Wizard.
    • Logic: (User.Languages.includes(Project.Source)) AND (User.Languages.includes(Project.Target))
  2. Availability Gate:
    • The speaker must be "Active" and not currently engaged in a maximum number of concurrent sessions.
  3. Performance Gate:
    • Speakers with a Flagged status are automatically excluded.
    • Speakers with a Star Rating < 3.0 are deprioritized or excluded based on PM settings.
